author: ROME | Ghaz OutroONI
content:
I know I’m left curve IQ but I always thought of it like I’m an investor buying assets for the digital economy/metaverse whatever we want to call it. Not necessarily an investor in the team directly. Staked polis is like being a shareholder in the dao and the dao more or less contracts the building part out to the team. Which is why we always joked about being able to fire or replace the team in the far future. Not that I’d want to they are killing it right now. 

But like Euga already said this is the place to talk to the team about the game we’d like to see built which is in our interests since we have stakes in the dao and in the metaverse but not the team themselves. We are lucky that they give us this level of access as it is and should appreciate what’s happening here. 

Also please no more gated or whale chats. This is plenty and I’m happy it got opened up as read only to everyone.

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
What DeepSeek thinks about Star Atlas (relevant 2023):

Star Atlas is a highly ambitious blockchain-based game that blends space exploration, NFTs, and decentralized governance. Here’s a balanced breakdown of its key risks and potential future outlook:
Key Risks to Consider

Market Volatility & Speculation:  ,- In-game assets (ships, land, currency) are tied to cryptocurrencies (e.g., $ATLAS, $POLIS), making their value vulnerable to crypto market swings.Speculative bubbles could form, leading to asset price crashes if hype fades or adoption stalls.,
Development Challenges:  ,- The game’s AAA-quality vision is still in early development (as of 2023). Delays, technical hurdles, or underdelivered features (common in complex games) could erode trust.Balancing gameplay depth with blockchain mechanics is tricky—too much focus on "play-to-earn" risks turning it into a grind rather than fun.,
Economic Sustainability:  ,- Play-to-earn models rely on a steady influx of new players to sustain rewards. If growth stalls, earnings could dry up (see Axie Infinity’s 2022 crash).Wealth concentration among early investors might create imbalances, alienating casual players.,
Regulatory Uncertainty:  ,- Governments are scrutinizing NFTs and crypto. Bans or restrictions in key markets (e.g., the EU, U.S.) could limit accessibility or liquidity of assets.Tax implications for NFT trades or earnings could complicate player participation.,
Complexity & Accessibility:  ,- Requires familiarity with crypto wallets, NFTs, and blockchain transactions—a barrier for mainstream gamers.High upfront costs (e.g., buying NFTs to play competitively) could deter newcomers.,
Security Risks:  ,- Blockchain games are targets for hacks and exploits. A major breach could destabilize the in-game economy or erode trust.
---

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
Future Perspectives & Opportunities
 
Innovation in Gaming: , - If executed well, Star Atlas could set a new standard for blockchain games, merging AAA-quality visuals with player-driven economies.Features like DAO governance (players voting on game direction) and a living metaverse could attract a loyal community.,
Growing Blockchain Gaming Market:  ,- The blockchain gaming sector is projected to grow rapidly. Star Atlas could capitalize on this trend, especially with Solana’s scalability.
Strong Community & Partnerships: , - Active Discord/Twitter communities and partnerships with crypto platforms suggest robust ecosystem support.Integration with DeFi platforms could enable unique earning opportunities (e.g., staking NFTs for yield).,
Metaverse Integration,:  - As interest in the metaverse grows, Star Atlas’s persistent universe could become a hub for social interaction, commerce, and cross-game NFT utility.
Environmental Edge:  ,- Built on Solana, which uses energy-efficient proof-of-history, addressing ESG concerns about blockchain’s carbon footprint.

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
---  Critical Factors for Success
 
Gameplay First: The game must prioritize fun and engagement over monetization to retain players long-term.,Economic Balance: Avoiding hyperinflation or deflation in the in-game economy will require careful design and active governance.,Regulatory Compliance: Adapting to global crypto regulations will be crucial for mainstream adoption.,Community Trust: Transparent communication and delivering on promises will determine whether players stay invested.,
---

Conclusion

Star Atlas has the potential to revolutionize blockchain gaming by merging high-quality visuals, deep strategy, and decentralized ownership. However, its success hinges on overcoming significant technical, economic, and regulatory challenges. For players and investors, it offers high-risk, high-reward opportunities—ideal for those comfortable with crypto’s volatility and drawn to pioneering projects. Watch for updates on development milestones, player adoption, and regulatory shifts to gauge its long-term viability.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content: Yeah FF for sure. My question is more about potentially destabilizing the market.  If FF dumps tons of ships for rent, SAGE players won't be able to compete with that price (whatever is higher than they get in FF), which will dilute assets.  Ownership value will go down, atlas value will go down. Maybe i don't see the full picture, but that's how i feel about current plan as it is. Renting can be super damaging to economy, it should be designed as separate complex game loop

author: [SAI] Torinder ⪛๏⫺ [SKR]
content: Hey @[SAI] Torinder ⪛๏⫺ , I'm happy to answer your questions.  Return on VWAP: Here we measure the net profit from all of your gameloops * 365 in relation to your VWAP. There are two metrics, one is from yesterday and the other one is the average over the last week. There are two downsides to this:  1) We are using VWAP and not market prizes right now. We plan the offer this metric also with market prizes as it will show a more accurate yield on your invested capital. 2) VWAP does not include any crew, as we are still not able to track crew inside SAGE Labs with flipside. So if your second profile has a lot of crew and is doing crafting or upgrading, the return on vwap can get pretty high. Still, we think there is some value to this. The best one is probably to compare your result from yesterday with your weekly average, as non of the problems matter in the comparison as they are the same for both. You could also do some carefully comparisons with other player profile if you keep in mind the crew will impact the numbers.  Profit Margin: The profit margin is calculated by dividing your net profit with your total revenue. A profit margin of 30% means, that 70% of your revenue are costs and 30% are net profit.  You want both metrics to be as high as possible. But a profit margin of 48% is crazy high already. I would assume that in this player profile you are not doing any crafting or upgrading as those two gameloops offer a very high ROI combined with a low profit margin. For scanning and mining it is the other way around. ROI is lower as for crafting and upgrading, but the profit margins are much higher.   You could call crafting & upgrading a 'low margin business' with all its advantages and disadvantages. It means that price fluctuations have a very high impact on your returns. One day you can make a huge profit and the next day it's a loss if the prize moves fast.

author: mikatachan | [ROME]
content: Yes, the revenue is calculated with the average price of the day you mined the resource,  you found the SDU or you crafted the recipe. It is not measuring the cash-flow when you trade them. Right now we are using the prices from the Galactic Marketplace. We would like to switch to Local Markets as soon as the liquidity is high enough.

It is not measuring the cash-flow when you trade them. Right now we are using the prices from the Galactic Marketplace. We would like to switch to Local Markets as soon as the liquidity is high enough.

author: questionoak
content:
I am moving an idea I mentioned in the DAO chat here to get the thoughts of the dev team. I am concerned about the long term problem of VWAP (or ship pricing). This is probably too long a conversation to have typing on discord.

Here is my logic and you guys can tell me your thoughts...
VWAP at the time it was established in the first Galactic Offering was certainly related to the value of the dollar ~four years ago. The value of the dollar has gone down 24% over that time. The VWAP is the closest the digital economy has to a number related to the real-world cost to create that digital asset from idea to fully functional 3d model. By that I mean the real-world time (cost) to create everything for an airbike is significantly less than a C11. (I do expect AI will greatly reduce the costs, but in the end the bigger the ship... the more to build the models)
I am suggesting we implement some CPI in the economy maybe tied to VWAP (??) or we risk the economy selling ships down the road that might never get actually made in the digital world. I am already concerned we do not have anywhere near enough funds to build what we have already sold.
I think of VWAP like MSRP of a car. Over the past four years... Toyota Camry  MSRP $25,045, $25,395, $25,945, $26,320. I would add that I think they are not staying current with inflation either. When a new model year comes out, the price goes up almost always but the deaelers always have old inventory. Because the new models cost more, it helps them sell the old inventory. I think in our economy, it might help hold up the value of vehicles already purchased as well when people know that if they wait the cost will be more.

author: [AEP] Mandalorian (Djin Djarin)
content:
Dear Economics Team,

I listened to the recent report meeting and found it insightful overall. However, If I understand it correctly concerns regarding the proposal to allow users to build ships in a free-to-play environment and then transfer these assets into the main game. Please correct me if I’m wrong, but this approach could be perceived as devaluing the investments made by early contributors to the project. 

Since the launch of Sage, we have yet to address several critical in-game issues, including UI/UX problems and the fee structure, which require immediate attention. The skepticism about the SA team's focus is not unfounded, as there has been no significant appreciation in the value of GAO original assets post-launch. So doing this would possibly further devalue the original assets?

While the idea of using a test bed for development has its merits, we must be cautious about its implications, particularly how it might affect our existing user base and the integrity of the main game's ecosystem. 

I appreciate the innovation this approach brings, but urge a thorough evaluation of its impact before proceeding.
@chrisks.game.economy

However, If I understand it correctly concerns regarding the proposal to allow users to build ships in a free-to-play environment and then transfer these assets into the main game. Please correct me if I’m wrong, but this approach could be perceived as devaluing the investments made by early contributors to the project. Since the launch of Sage, we have yet to address several critical in-game issues, including UI/UX problems and the fee structure, which require immediate attention. The skepticism about the SA team's focus is not unfounded, as there has been no significant appreciation in the value of GAO original assets post-launch. So doing this would possibly further devalue the original assets? While the idea of using a test bed for development has its merits, we must be cautious about its implications, particularly how it might affect our existing user base and the integrity of the main game's ecosystem. I appreciate the innovation this approach brings, but urge a thorough evaluation of its impact before proceeding. @chrisks.game.economy

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
From my perspective Sage is playing is far better than it ever has been, and that includes the vastly new complicated game components, e.g. local markets and FICs.  I haven't had a FIC sell transaction fail in over 2 weeks and mostly under 5 sec confirmation time.  And that's using the certificate system which has 2x transactions.

I regularly use the local market to buy and sell carbon, fuel, hydrogen, + others, and it executives flawlessly.  In fact better than the GM, which makes sense given the newer technology.

The newest addition of the priority fees toggle has been a much requested feature which should help out in times of extreme congestion.
And the tx manager is something that I absolutely love but didn't know I needed. Keeping the dev console open breaks immersion and now I may not need to do that. Love keeping it in game.

There are of course still improvements to make, but the recent changes and fixes have shown a razor sharp focus on improving game playability and qol for users.   Exactly what we need right now.

content: If i mined everything myself, i dunno if i would take the value of the materials as the cost of the ship. wouldn't it just be my actual costs up to that point (tx fees, gas, etc)? it kinda feels like double counting

author: mikatachan | [ROME]
content: Yes, the c is crafting and the ca is cargo.   Well, if we wouldn't use the price of the raw materials as costs for crafting we would have a double count. Because this profit is already in your mining business.  Crafting is only adding value if the price of the crafted asset is higher than the price of the ingredients and the crafting fee.

reply.preview: Yes, the c is crafting and the ca is cargo. Well, if we wouldn't use the price of the raw materials as costs for crafting we would have a double count. author: [AEP] Viktor [AEP]
content:
Hmm, I think you're mixing it up a bit.

Net profit for mining: Price of resources mined - operating costs for mining fleet - txs costs
Net profit for crafting: Price of crafted asset - price of ingredients - crafting fee - txs fee
That way there is no double count, as the output of mining is the input for crafting.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
Yeah, but don't quote me on that—I only have some basic information about blockchain compared to others here! I'm much more gaming-oriented; that's my golden spot, XD, and that's why I understand your concerns.

I think that as more new features are implemented, the necessity for Apollo becomes more evident. It all depends on the needs of a project: you could easily bring a chess game onto the blockchain with no downsides, but you could never do the same for, say, poker (fully on-chain). To hide information, you'll need something else, I think.

There are a bunch of talented people in ATMTA; I'm 100% sure they will find the right way to do so. It might take some time, but we'll get there eventually!

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
Functional fleet command consisting of feature parity with Starbased should come at about the same time as C4 on Apollo as a PTR (testing server). C4 will stay in testing as long as necessary, but we will also work on bringing Fleet Command up to parity with C4 over that time. There will be a number of additional updates to UE over this time frame as well. 

Free to play version of SAGE on Apollo is also looking insanely promising. Much sooner than people think.

This concern about combat is overblown. It will be in public testing before mainnet release. And requesting more nuanced systems like ship configuration misses the point that combat is a core pillar of a 4X game. Exploitation. C4 gets us to a full 4X/RTS game. PVE is way more difficult onchain than PVP. We need to execute this year.

author: [AEP] Mandalorian (Djin Djarin)
content: v3.1.90 has a new toggle in the settings pane that should disable other fleets background data streams. For those experiencing lag or who want to conserve data, this should make a significant improvement. When you enable this toggle, you can no longer see any other fleets on the Galaxy view.   The existing "Show All Fleets" toggle (on the Galaxy Map) is a UI only element. Turning it off doesn't stop the flow of data.

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
@SW4GN3R @Chri.z 
Curious if we can make the ATLAS Locker a near automatic and invisible service, yet create a better UI/UX directly in SAGE or Faction Fleet that easily shows players its benefits, savings and earning in real time?

Example:
-The ATLAS Lockers is built directly in SAGE
-A pop-up confirmation window is shown when a new player starts playing SAGE letting them know about the ATLAS Locker perks
-Have a display bar in game showing progress to the next ATLAS Locker Tier
-When a player reaches the first tier of the ATLAS Locker plus x amount over for the player to easily keep playing SA, a pop-up window comes up asking the players if they would like to lock their ATLAS to receive X perks?
-Have an easy to understand UI/UX dashboard for the ATLAS Locker in SAGE/Showroom that shows the different items they gain, the amount they have saved/earned
-If a player does not opt into locking, these players still have access to the ATLAS Locker dashboard but it shows the amount of savings/earnings that they have missed out on & maybe a in-game prompt comes up once a week directing the player to the dashboard to show them what they are missing out on
-Create the option for community built tools to be able to add custom perks for locking ATLAS (ATMTA could recommend that the expenses for this be funded by the DAO through a PIP)
-To locker ATLAS players should not have to break immersion & it should be one click

content:

-If ATLAS is unlocked the discount timer is reset

author: ROME | Ghaz OutroONI
content: Absolutely. It will need to go through the formal PIP process, including submitting it initially as a PIP Idea. ATMTA is in the fortunate position that we can seed our ideas more publicly because we have the full community attention. All specific details will need to be sorted through the documented ideation phase, and then submitted for vote.  Separately, I saw your comments on X and wanted to reinforce that yes, the current authority of the DAO exclusively surrounds Treasury management. That's just the current phase we're in. And it's honestly pretty significant given the balance and what can be accomplished there. ATMTA still controls all tokenomics and game design otherwise.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
Pay over time is a separate topic, and not included in the Fleet Rental system. PoT is essentially a ship financing option that let's buyers take possession of a ship/fleet (via profile), use that ship to produce income, and make periodic payments. I like the idea, particularly after we get Council Rank online.,
Perhaps I didn't communicate this previously, but we did always intend to have a cancellation option for renters. The nice thing about the way this platform works is that cancelled fleets will be automatically relisted with the existing pre-set parameters.,

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: Here's the 2 YUGE risk IMO:  Ship values get rekt:, All ships get RENTED. Margins per ship dwindle significantly Value per ship goes down. APR is the main driver of ship values.  Economics of buying new ship gets destroyed  The only saving grace in that scenario is if the value of $ATLAS would go up significantly  Small to mid size players get REKT:,  What happens if me, Bodhi and Mando rent EVERYTHING? The entire player base is REKT. The META is going to be: RENT EVERYTHING "everything" is an exaggeration but you get the point  The bigger concern is losing a good portion of the small to mid size players. I don't see a scenario where they don't get crushed Their earnings get slashed.  They quit.  Power will aggregate to the whales  And I am a whale, but I care more about the overall health of the ecosystem  If I was in your shoes Michael, I would have waited for CR before releasing rentals.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: You have to remember that there is still limited inventory across the market. Every asset has a fixed supply. And I'm anticipating Fleet Rentals to drive demand for ship ownership, not the opposite. Passive income is a powerful drug. This isn't a traditional game with unlimited supply of assets. The assets need to come from somewhere. And even in the case where we provide some liquidity for locker provisions (which drives revenue to ATLAS locker participants or the DAO), it's not some unchecked amount.

author: ZiggyMUD
content: That's one of the concerns: people could just rent from ATMTA instead of buying ships. We have a good amount of owned idle ships already. I can't justify it to myself why unsold inventory should be rented out.  It creates a very easy risk-free revenue path, that will potentially inflate the economy.  And of course I'd look into renting fleets myself, but I'm really not worried about my personal earnings in this discussion, but rather the sentiment/impact this move would create in the project.  Axie anxiety, maybe? Yes. But with good reasons to worry about it. And most important, because we're so passionate about this project... We really don't want to see it suffering.

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
Deepseek about ship rentals:

Impact of Affordable Ship Rentals on Star Atlas' Economy and Associated Risks
The introduction of affordable ship rentals in Star Atlas—both for player-owned and developer-owned unsold ships—could significantly reshape the game’s economy. Below is an analysis of potential impacts and risks, based on the game’s existing mechanics and blockchain-driven structure.

Positive Economic Impacts
Lower Entry Barriers for New Players

High upfront costs for ships currently limit accessibility. Affordable rentals would democratize access, enabling more players to participate in resource gathering, exploration, and combat without heavy initial investments. This could expand the player base and stimulate economic activity.

Increased Resource Liquidity

More players with access to ships would accelerate resource extraction and trade, potentially boosting the in-game economy. For example, resources like fuel, ammunition, and raw materials could see higher circulation, driving market dynamism.

New Revenue Streams

Ship owners (players and developers) could earn passive income by renting out their NFTs. This aligns with existing mechanics. Developers could also monetize unsold ships, funding further development .

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
Risks and Challenges
Over-Saturation and Asset Depreciation

Flooding the market with rental ships might devalue ship NFTs, especially rare or high-tier models. Early investors who purchased ships at premium prices could face losses if demand for ownership declines.

Resource Inflation and Scarcity

Increased ship availability could lead to over-extraction of resources, creating negative externalities such as scarcity and price volatility. For instance, excessive mining in a region might deplete resources for neighboring players, destabilizing local economies.

Income Inequality and Power Imbalances

Wealthy players or guilds with large fleets could dominate rental markets, centralizing control over resources and pricing. This might create a "landlord" dynamic, where casual players struggle to compete.

Shift in Gameplay Incentives

If rentals become the primary focus, players might prioritize profit over exploration or factional objectives, undermining the game’s strategic and social depth. For example, guilds might focus on leasing ships instead of participating in faction wars.


Conclusion
Affordable ship rentals could democratize Star Atlas’ economy, fostering growth and inclusivity. However, unchecked implementation risks inflation, inequality, and gameplay distortion. Success hinges on balancing accessibility with sustainable economic design, ensuring that rentals complement—rather than undermine—the game’s vision of a player-driven metaverse

author: ROME | Zamsor ⪛⦿⫺ [ROME]
content: Deepseek about ship rentals:  Impact of Affordable Ship Rentals on Star Atlas' Economy and Associated Risks The introduction of affordable ship rentals in Star Atlas—both for player-owned and developer-owned unsold ships—could significantly reshape the game’s economy. Below is an analysis of potential impacts and risks, based on the game’s existing mechanics and blockchain-driven structure.  Positive Economic Impacts Lower Entry Barriers for New Players  High upfront costs for ships currently limit accessibility. Affordable rentals would democratize access, enabling more players to participate in resource gathering, exploration, and combat without heavy initial investments. This could expand the player base and stimulate economic activity.  Increased Resource Liquidity  More players with access to ships would accelerate resource extraction and trade, potentially boosting the in-game economy. For example, resources like fuel, ammunition, and raw materials could see higher circulation, driving market dynamism.  New Revenue Streams  Ship owners (players and developers) could earn passive income by renting out their NFTs. This aligns with existing mechanics. Developers could also monetize unsold ships, funding further development .

author: Rick Moonkin [STAR]
content:
Unbalanced Power Dynamics,
Risk: Developers could prioritize renting high-tier ships , giving renters temporary advantages in battles or resource gathering.

Consequence: Casual players or small guilds might struggle to compete, creating a "pay-to-rent" imbalance.

Conflict of Interest,
Risk: Developers might artificially limit ship sales to push rentals, prioritizing recurring revenue over player ownership.

Consequence: Players could perceive the system as exploitative, damaging trust in the project’s decentralized vision.


Mitigation Strategies
Dynamic Rental Caps: Limit the number of unsold ships available for rent based on player population or resource scarcity.

Ownership Incentives: Offer exclusive benefits (e.g., higher $POLIS staking rewards) to players who purchase ships outright.

Transparent Supply Rules: Publicly track unsold ship inventories to prevent artificial scarcity manipulation.

Resource Sinks: Introduce mechanics (e.g., ship maintenance costs) to counter inflation from increased resource extraction.

Conclusion
Renting unsold developer-owned ships could democratize access to Star Atlas’ economy but risks centralization, inflation, and player distrust. Success depends on balancing rentals with ownership incentives and maintaining transparency to uphold the game’s decentralized ethos.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
My 2 atlas on hot topic:
An important part of the success of Star Atlas is its community. There is an obvious difference in the behavior of a player who has invested in a project and a player who uses a rented ships. 
The current game economy is not optimal due to the huge difference in assets between the largest and medium/small players, as well as a limited number of players in general. If you analyze the actions of the largest players, you will often see that people certainly generate profit, but they do not abuse the system to the maximum, each player decides for himself where this line is, someone leaves the LP system in favor of contracts, although the economic benefit of this is not obvious, others continue to commit the SDU into the Basses, although the price at the moment may not be optimal, there are players who fundamentally use local markets and pay commissions instead of OTC. We are lucky to have worthy players in the community, but in addition to a high moral standard, they are also pressured by the assets that they hold in their portfolio. 
On the other side if players with 99% rented ships start coming to the game. These players will be extractors, they have no reason not to abuse the system, they will come - squeeze the juices when the atlas grows and return the rented fleets with 1 button when the economy stagnates. 
Extractors will not use Atlas Locker, they will use free liquidity to scale fleets, and then run away to another "hype" project or mem token. On the other hand asset holders will use locker because it is profitable in the long terms
I agree with those who propose to establish limits on the number of rented ships, so that every player on there from zero to hero path will be forced to buy assets. As an example - managing a rented fleet requires twice as many consul rank points in comparison of own assets, or the total amount of rented ships cannot exceed 200% of the total cost of the fleet that you own in WWAP terms.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
Renting unsold inventory is potentially, by far, the change that would cause the biggest economical impact among everything we've been presented with.
It should be a big "no". It makes absolutely no sense from any economical perspective, except for one: providing initial rental liquidity.
Guess what we have to provide liquidity with? Score. Shut Faction Fleet down, and give it a few days. All passive players will rent out their fleets, and liquidity will be there.

Artificially inflating the economy with assets that haven't been properly introduced into it (as in, purchased) carry an enormous risk to destabilize everything. Am I being doomsday? Maybe. But there are very strong arguments for me to do so. And I'm not alone in this.

If you analyze the actions of the largest players, you will often see that people certainly generate profit, but they do not abuse the system to the maximum, each player decides for himself where this line is, someone leaves the LP system in favor of contracts, although the economic benefit of this is not obvious, others continue to commit the SDU into the Basses, although the price at the moment may not be optimal, there are players who fundamentally use local markets and pay commissions instead of OTC. We are lucky to have worthy players in the community, but in addition to a high moral standard, they are also pressured by the assets that they hold in their portfolio.  On the other side if players with 99% rented ships start coming to the game. These players will be extractors, they have no reason not to abuse the system, they will come - squeeze the juices when the atlas grows and return the rented fleets with 1 button when the economy stagnates.  Extractors will not use Atlas Locker, they will use free liquidity to scale fleets, and then run away to another "hype" project or mem token. On the other hand asset holders will use locker because it is profitable in the long terms I agree with those who propose to establish limits on the number of rented ships, so that every player on there from zero to hero path will be forced to buy assets. As an example - managing a rented fleet requires twice as many consul rank points in comparison of own assets, or the total amount of rented ships cannot exceed 200% of the total cost of the fleet that you own in WWAP terms.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
Right. For every ship sold, there will obviously exist a new owner. Who may or may not have interest in supplying those on the rental market. And given demand for ships could skyrocket through the rental market, what happens when there are no fleets available to rent for the people that sold, but still intend to play? They'll be out of luck, and have zero earnings potential. Hence, the value of capital asset ownership is retained.

I've compared Fleet Rentals to real estate on several occasions. Real estate is one of the largest industries globally. With finite supply capital assets (like our ships/fleets, and real estate), ownership is sought after. In much the same way you can rent a property for far less than the cost of purchasing, purchasing demand still exists.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
Not quite. This ties into the ATLAS Locker Addendum proposal that would enable a liquidity provision of some reasonable amount of Fleets into the Fleet Rental system as a way to seed initial liquidity, using unsold inventory of ATMTA ships. The quantity of those fleets would be released on a controlled basis, but could ultimately scale based on the total value (in some USD equivalent terms) of ATLAS locked in the locker. With more ATLAS locked, more fleets could be unlocked through the program. The revenues from those rentals would be distributed to ATLAS Locker participants on a proportional basis, or through some combination of DAO and ATLAS Locker participant distributions, driving further incentive to participate in the locker. ATMTA would make zero revenue from these features.

Unfortunately, there are exaggerated concerns that we're just going to release an "unlimited" supply of new ships onto the rental platform. This wouldn't be the case. My primary concern is that we see too little supply on the Fleet Rental system, diminishing impact of the product release. In terms of being able to provide concrete numbers, I simply can't provide any specific detail right now, because we will have to see what Fleet Rental marketplace activity looks like on launch. And I would also like to see if, and how many, ships/fleets transition from Faction Fleet into the rental system after launch.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
That said, we are still here in the open discussion period of these proposed features. As we noted on the brew, and have repeated multiple times, none of these ATLAS locker enhancements need to be released if there isn't support for them. In terms of seeding fleets, what I'd really like to see is an opportunity to open up for potential new players to experience Star Atlas without needing to make the full capital commitment of purchasing large scale fleets. I do genuinely believe that the current capital requirements to get into SA are an impediment to growth. And some light analysis (supported by Aephia / @[AEP] Viktor ) suggests that minimum profitable fleet size is in the $250-$500 range, which is more than any other "video game." And while that isn't a tremendous amount of money in the crypto space, it is a lot in a lot of regions in the world. This is in addition to the complexity of playing SAGE in the first place. We're tackling these issues through separate initiatives, including work on tutorial systems, tooltips, educational video content, work with content creators, a new fully free-to-play version of SAGE (which I'm testing this weekend -- and it's pretty sick), alongside the possibility to enable fleet rentals at a fraction of the cost so people can at least experiment with the live economy on mainnet before spending a considerable sum of money on ships. 

This top of funnel growth could and should lead to conversions of players, who ultimately will want to buy and own ships. And the Fleet Rental system also brings potential of attracting a new "investor" class of participants who want to own ships specifically because passive income opportunities exist through rental markets. 

Kind of like REITS (real estate investment trusts). Maybe we should create a SHEIT (ship estate investment trust).

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Renting unsold inventory is potentially, by far, the change that would cause the biggest economical impact among everything we've been presented with. It should be a big "no". It makes absolutely no sense from any economical perspective, except for one: providing initial rental liquidity. Guess what we have to provide liquidity with? Score. Shut Faction Fleet down, and give it a few days. All passive players will rent out their fleets, and liquidity will be there.  Artificially inflating the economy with assets that haven't been properly introduced into it (as in, purchased) carry an enormous risk to destabilize everything. Am I being doomsday? Maybe. But there are very strong arguments for me to do so. And I'm not alone in this.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
We don't have to force it. The natural limiting factor will be Council Rank, in addition to availability of supply. Again, if people think they can just sell everything and 20x their fleet, they are going to be sorely mistaken. If there is that much demand in the market for rentals, the fleets won't be available to use. We aren't going to see some magical 20x increase in the amount of ships available. They're all fixed supply. Even if we were to add some supply of unsold ships via the locker program, it's going to be a fraction of what already exists out there.

Now in terms of waiting for CR, I believe that to be unnecessary. We're progressing towards our goals of C4 quite nicely, and I know our projected timelines for that release. And it's going to take some time for rental markets to really get activated. I'm comfortable with that window of time existing, in which there is no inherent cap on rentals, before C4 comes out.

author: defiantwyvern
content:
for fleet rentals, how will passenger crews be handled? Seems like a potential issue, b/c crew are assets and tracked at the port of entry per account, and not associated specifically with ships.

e.g. - a rental consumer rents a ship, loads up 5 of their crew on a ship, then defaults on their rental payment. The rentral contract is terminated, but the crew on the ship are assets that are owned by the rental consumer, not the rental provider. But now they are stuck in the rental provider ship.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
People renting out fleets will just have to include a 300 Atlas extra to cover potential self-destructs.

It should be launched with this feature, a "self-destruct" deposit, that is returned only if the rental ends at the place the fleets started at. Easy solution.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: And also add a disclaimer/reminder when rental is about to end: "please make sure you unloaded all personal items/crew/virtual girlfriends from the fleet before the timer is up".

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: I would also like a toggle feature that prevents the contract to be terminated prematurely. That could create tiers of rentals. As in, more expensive, but you can terminate it before contract is over. And a locked period, but then the one providing the fleet knows it will last for its entirety, and could charge slightly cheaper (market would balance itself out, probably...).

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Therefore my suggestion was to make the time for the free respawn ultra long. If the respawn time is 8 or 12 hours, yes, you would save fuel, but you would waste A LOT of time. And because time is money, it would be better to just produce the fuel and warp normally. Another idea would be: You can lock some fuel and in case your fleet needs to respawn. Then you can just use the locked fuel for it. Another idea: Let us use another fleet to transfer fuel to the fleet (like a rescue mission), so we don't need a SB to transfer fuel.

content:
@SW4GN3R couple ideas for user retention, and bringing more people in
create an item in game that is craftable that is a one time use to record your time in the racing game ue5. users can race for free any earn nothing, or buy/craft these "tickets" and use them, in which case their race time will be recorded on the leaderboard, and there will be a 1st through 5th prize at the end of every week, and a leaderboard reset.,use the same ticket system for a deathmatch style arena competition. users can play for free, and earn nothing or go into a higher stakes lobby with a ticket entry fee, and theres some prizes for top place,do the same with the pve shooter, pve ship combat, and deathmatch racing,create nft weapons, armors, and grenades, (racing ship stuff too) that users can grind rng for, win as prizes, or craft in game (maybe even exchange sage exp for, or unlock/upgarade),create a circulating economy where some of these items are expendable, or even breakable (grenades/ammo/special ammo) armor breaks ect.,further circulate by creating an exchange system where multiple items can be burned with a guarantee of an upgraded item (can even just be cosmetic) and a small chance to create a rare version.,create rgn based drop mechanics where certain items are only obtainable in specific ue5 or sage game modes. (have a chance to find ship parts while scanning in sage, have a chance to find special weapons while playing pve, chance for ship upgrades in race mode.,give a loot box for each game mode based on a minimum number of pve kills, or races won, or waves completed. (if you beat wave 5 in the pve ship combat 10 times you get a loot box) or for every 100 enemies killed in the pve shooter ect) can also be given as a weekly prize mentioned in #1, 2, and 3.,have these loot boxes tradable, so people can speculate on them or open them for a guarntee of small items like ammo, grenades, and a progressivly small chance at rare, and useful items that can increate the speed that you get these chests (by being able to kill pve shooters faster with a better weapon or attachment for example),have specific loot boxes for each game mode where certain rare items are only obtainable by opening boxes.,create tradable base parts for some of these items to be able to drop parts of an item, then you have to collect them through varius game modes and loot boxes to complete the item. those completed items can then be used in the upgrading system to continue circulating the underlying peices.,

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content:
create a max supply on some of these rare items to incentivize grinding early, and release items over time that play off each other and create new an exciting combos (stun grenade to sniper rifle) but if you find a regen armor thats better at keeping your shield topped off you might run smg, or shotgun...  future items changing the combos also giving future players something to grind for.,create a limit on how many times an item can be attempted to upgrade (burn to upgrade mechanic in #6), this will create an imposed scarcity on the low chance rare items, and incentivize users to take a risk on loosing multiple items for a chance at the best exclusive items. if they loose the roll they still get an ipgraded item but it should be at a loss unless they get lucky.,tie progression to these mechanics. giving users who put in the time to primary access to finding/building these items. if players want to "pay to win" they have to pay a user who worked for the asset rather that buy it from the team.,create various difficulties for each pve game modes, and rng scales, high level items can only drop at the places where you need upgraded items to complete.,the primary advantage to these assets should be in the ability to progress faster in pve, and find more items (by increased speed to get the lootbox requirements, compete in the leaderboards, or play more difficult variants. the advantages in pvp should be noticeable, but balanced. where as the pve aspect should scale considerably to be able to create a meaningful progression system.,tie exp in this somehow to where paying to win only gives so much advantage. players should want to put in the time, not just the money. if the money can help get you lvled up faster thats fine. but it shouldn't be a requirement.,create "virtual" versions of mid to high level items that are lost on death, can be crafted in sage, and have a chance to break. so that free to play players can still compete at high levels. Where as the "collectable" variants are blockchain, more powerful, exceedingly rare, and only obtainable through chance over time, and permanent assets unless you take a chance on the exchange/upgrade),

we have such a cool game in ue5, if there was some more in depth integration with the on chain items, and some sort of progression/goal system so many people would play it all day while keeping an eye on sly.

all these items can be on the rental system too. many "whales" aren't the best racers, and shooters. they'd rather rent these items, out to players who can use them but might not be able or don't want to afford them.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: the basic premise is to create independent systems that benefit players, by having items and loops/incentives that are totally separated from atmta sales. (can still collect on tax from trades tho) if users want to pay their way to the best items they have to buy them from someone who played the game, and that person sets the price if he's the only one with an item early on. this will create more a time and effort based valuation on items rather than an atlas based valuation which will help when token prices are lower. if it took someone 20 hours to find one of the best items, and hes one of only a few people to have it hes not going to sell it for less even tho the markets down. hell just use it to find more items, and upgrade his gameplay.

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content: Has anyone looked at the likes of omniparser v2 for client-side SAGE automation? This version just came out, but screen understanding tools (in essence a screen reader hooked to LLM's to allow it to view and take action) seem to be interesting. Rote/scripted automation is great for routine things like mining, freighting, etc. When we have more dynamic things such as events, combat, etc., that will require reactions 24/7. Curious if SA would consider something like omniparser "too" automated...?

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: They will still be finite supply like our other assets. At least with respect to ATMTA mint. But we only minted a small quantity for Start Sequence. We don't have the numbers refined on what the total mint will look like, and we've also been discussing whether or not we follow a slightly updated structure for asset supply released going forward. Kind of a deep rabbit hole, but I want to make sure people know if/when more supply will exist, even if the entire supply isn't listed at one time (so we can more closely release assets alongside growth). Nevertheless, we'll communicate all of that publicly before we release anymore inventory for direct sales. And then, of course, just like with ships, players will eventually be able to craft their own claim stakes -- or at least craft components and inputs that go into forming claim stakes.

reply.preview: They will still be finite supply like our other assets. At least with respect to ATMTA mint. But we only minted a small quantity for Start Sequence. We don't have the numbers refined on what the total mint will look like, and we've also been discussing whether or not we follow a slightly updated structure for asset supply released going forward. Kind of a deep rabbit hole, but I want to make sure people know if/when more supply will exist, even if the entire supply isn't listed at one time (so we can more closely release assets alongside growth). Nevertheless, we'll communicate all of that publicly before we release anymore inventory for direct sales. And then, of course, just like with ships, players will eventually be able to craft their own claim stakes -- or at least craft components and inputs that go into forming claim stakes.

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: @bunthius will claimstakes be able to be taken from T1->T5 or will there be an upgrade cap based on initial tier?

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: They will still be finite supply like our other assets. At least with respect to ATMTA mint. But we only minted a small quantity for Start Sequence. We don't have the numbers refined on what the total mint will look like, and we've also been discussing whether or not we follow a slightly updated structure for asset supply released going forward. Kind of a deep rabbit hole, but I want to make sure people know if/when more supply will exist, even if the entire supply isn't listed at one time (so we can more closely release assets alongside growth). Nevertheless, we'll communicate all of that publicly before we release anymore inventory for direct sales. And then, of course, just like with ships, players will eventually be able to craft their own claim stakes -- or at least craft components and inputs that go into forming claim stakes.

reply.preview: They will still be finite supply like our other assets. At least with respect to ATMTA mint. But we only minted a small quantity for Start Sequence. We don't have the numbers refined on what the total mint will look like, and we've also been discussing whether or not we follow a slightly updated structure for asset supply released going forward. Kind of a deep rabbit hole, but I want to make sure people know if/when more supply will exist, even if the entire supply isn't listed at one time (so we can more closely release assets alongside growth). Nevertheless, we'll communicate all of that publicly before we release anymore inventory for direct sales. And then, of course, just like with ships, players will eventually be able to craft their own claim stakes -- or at least craft components and inputs that go into forming claim stakes.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content: it'll work similarly to claim stakes is why it's not talked about too much. i mean the system is similar but you have a plot/hab and in hab units like the crafting station and cargo storage instead of the plot/hub and buildings/modules that go on a productive plot. crafting habs gives you a certain amount of fee-free crafting slots on CSS/Starbases. There will be plots/habs on starbases as well where this system will work, which incentivizes ownership during territory disputes.

content:
@! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺ 

It's my understanding that EVENTUALLY  every game mechanic from mining to transport to whatever will have an UE5 version.

And in these UE5 version, there will be interactive game play where a skilled engaged player will be able to outproduce someone using automation.

But let's be real: that's YEARS away.  Until then, automation will rule most game mechanics.

The team has already said they want to build SLY style automations in Fleet Command so everyone will have parity.

Combat will favor the manual players.

author: Gary Sanchez (AD Veh) [STAR]
content: Depending also civilisation/manufacturers, oni ships brand such busan , use more omnidirectionnal thrusters (almost spherical in pulse for multi-axial rotation) , while mud ships are more mecanical based, more rationnal with complex mecanical landing gear or thrusters system still moving as much as we can for good manoeuvrability but more aeronautical in this way , fimbul more retro-old school but its making their charms too with hydraulic jacks attachment on thrusters to make them move like on packlite or turbines on lowbie ,  we tried to make each brand a bit unique in this way . Animation dept playing big role to make ships looks alive in a nexgen way  for star atlas , to really reinforce feeling of fun /responding/immersive ships at maximum specially for our racers more agile .

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
SAGE uses some web2 solutions to mitigate the quadrillions queries to RPC, which is very expensive. Sometimes this mechanism fall behind and it needs a good ol’ kick to catch up.. 
other automation tools can read directly on chain (hence they are way more less optimized) so they don’t suffer from this and you always see an “updated state”

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Oh yeah... Not saying it's easy. Especially from the coding perspective.  But a nice feature would be a fee exemption exclusively for freighting. Because in reality, items would not be flipping hands permanently.  Player A hands out 1 cargo hold to Player B, B delivers from base X to base Y, straight into Player A's hands. No flipping, just freighting. No sense to charge fees for this loop.  Would feel like a "job dashboard". Which Michael said is on the roadmap, but not prioritized. I feel like it should be moved up a bit in priority, it's such a nice potential feature.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: > Wealth redistribution does not have to disproportionately punish top (large fleet) players IF implemented correctly (e.g., progresive, dynamic, incetive-based models vs flat taxes/wealth removal).  EXAMPLE: In EVE Online, NPC taxes and resource depletion mechanics help redistribute wealth indirectly without penalizing wealthy players.   > Wealth distribution in Game Economics is rarely a bell curve...typically follows a power law distribution (small fraction of players control the vast majority of assets/wealth.   > The behavior you describe about wealthy players is not fixed...game design shift economic incentives so that high-wealth players do not hoard and low-wealth players have more avenues to success.  EXAMPLE: In EVE Online, large corporations are forced to reinvest their wealth in territory control and war efforts. If they hoard too much, they become vulnerable.   > Velocity of Money can be increase through mechanics that don't rely on the Rich holding onto power.  You could implement system where players are encouraged to engage in frequent trade, apply active decay mechanics, or institute fluctuating tax rates, contracts...etc.   Redistribution of Wealth IS NOT Kneecapping! It's all about regulating and balancing economic flow rather that playing robin hood and directly taking from the rich and giving to the poor.  Done right, Top Players Will Stay IF Their Economic Power is Incentivized Properly: Don't let them sit on capital, promote the roles that encourage reinvestment.   <You make me write too much!  >

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: > Wealth redistribution does not have to disproportionately punish top (large fleet) players IF implemented correctly (e.g., progresive, dynamic, incetive-based models vs flat taxes/wealth removal).  EXAMPLE: In EVE Online, NPC taxes and resource depletion mechanics help redistribute wealth indirectly without penalizing wealthy players.   > Wealth distribution in Game Economics is rarely a bell curve...typically follows a power law distribution (small fraction of players control the vast majority of assets/wealth.   > The behavior you describe about wealthy players is not fixed...game design shift economic incentives so that high-wealth players do not hoard and low-wealth players have more avenues to success.  EXAMPLE: In EVE Online, large corporations are forced to reinvest their wealth in territory control and war efforts. If they hoard too much, they become vulnerable.   > Velocity of Money can be increase through mechanics that don't rely on the Rich holding onto power.  You could implement system where players are encouraged to engage in frequent trade, apply active decay mechanics, or institute fluctuating tax rates, contracts...etc.   Redistribution of Wealth IS NOT Kneecapping! It's all about regulating and balancing economic flow rather that playing robin hood and directly taking from the rich and giving to the poor.  Done right, Top Players Will Stay IF Their Economic Power is Incentivized Properly: Don't let them sit on capital, promote the roles that encourage reinvestment.   <You make me write too much!  >

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: Point #3 is a natural mechanic of gameplay/ life that you’re taking as something else. This is exactly the economic flow type I’m describing wealthy players take a loss on peak returns and reinvest or take a lose margin and the other economic areas fill that gap/need.   #4 doesn’t really effect anything with regards to wealth or asset holdings. Higher wealth individuals will have means to have higher velocity of money than much more smaller players mainly by the mechanic i described earlier. Higher wealth players or larger players will sacrifice percentages of profits due to volume. They have higher velocity of money compared to lower wealth players. Lower wealth players dont exchange mine as fast. This margin is picked up by the smaller players that exchange that value as higher returns for them for more labor in the exchange.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Communism requires totalitarian regimes to be sustainable (since Marx vision was never realized) and eventually incorporates elements of capitalist doctrines.  Some individuals view the redistribution of wealth as anti-capitalist or socialist, but they fail to recognize that even the ‘King of Capitalism,’ the USA, practices it due to the inherent inequalities that pure capitalism can create. Redistribution of wealth is not inherently socialist; it exists in social democracies, mixed economies, and regulated capitalism. The USA follows a mixed-market economy with redistribution policies such as Social Security and others programs...It counters capitalism's disparities without eliminating private enterprise and investment.   Yes, let's openly experiment with more dynamic and innovative economic systems at the regional level to establish better standards of wealth and success. There are some interesting ideas to try like Special Economic Zones, worker cooperatives, Universal Basic Income, and participatory budgeting.   Sign me up.    CC:  @SW4GN3R

author: odvb
content: A balance between supporting innovation and ensuring a fair economy is key. Penalizing wealth creation can stifle growth, but unchecked wealth concentration can also lead to stagnation.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
From my perspective, opportunity exists for players of (almost) every size and scale right now. And that's what is most important. As long as people have the opportunity to participate in the economy and build wealth for themselves, they have an opportunity to prosper long term. We don't need to balance out wealth distribution, we need to balance opportunity.

We still have some work to do on the lower end of portfolio curve to ensure there are profitable loops. Perhaps the biggest unforeseen challenge (from my eyes) was the impact of Solana tx fees. I certainly never expected Solana fees to ever be an issue given their very low cost. But given the sheer quantity of transactions processed in SAGE, increased network volume, and the impact of priority fees, we are seeing a disproportionate share of gross profit going to these fees in our smaller users

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Yes, game taxation builds value for the DAO. People exploiting OTC systems to avoid paying transaction fees are directly stealing from everyone with a locked balance of POLIS. The DAO requires revenue sources, and value capture from game activity is the intended mechanism to accomplish that.   People are conflating future employment contracts with current functionality of the game. Arbitrage strategies (which is effectively what we have now) are different from freighting or crafting contracts. At some point those features will be released, and you can hire someone to take your resources from one location to another. Or you'll be able to hire someone to craft some items for you. You will still need to compensate them for that, as I doubt they're just going to be offering it to you for free out of the goodness of their heart. That cost will be a direct revenue source to the person providing the service. But when those systems are online, you won't have to pay the marketplace transaction fee twice, like you do now if you are trying to arbitrage local markets. In the latter case, anyone interested in arb needs to account for those marketplace fees. Same way it works if you're a market maker trying to arb any financial market. You can't just tell a CEX to cut your fees so you can trade profitably. In our case, we do offer the ATLAS locker, which is a tool to reduce your trade fees, and can open up arbitrage opportunities to you that don't exist for others who don't have a locked balance of ATLAS. And people utilizing the ATLAS locker actually benefit everyone else in the ecosystem by absorbing supply of ATLAS.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: Deep topic here. First, Social Security isn't actually a social program. People pay into Social Security from wages their entire lives, and receive benefits when reaching a requisite age. If anything, the American people paying in to the program are getting screwed with SS benefits, because the coffers have been pillaged for other programs. Comparing an enforced, individual savings and investment account performance against the perpetuity of SS, the individual would be much better off saving and investing on their own. Conceptually, the idea of the program was good, but it significantly underperforms individual retirement savings.  Separately, I think social welfare programs can also be a good thing. Unfortunately, we see these programs persistently abused by people participating in them, and money being both inefficiently or inappropriately used. Hence, why we have DOGE now in the US analyzing all of the spending, and an incredible amount of corruption surfacing as a result.  Systems that penalize wealth generation resulting from innovation and investment just discourage that behavior and introduces more dependents into the system looking for handouts. We should be rewarding enterprising individuals who are willing to take big risks in capital investment for the game.

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: BTW, taxation in SAGE/SA is not too dissimilar from taxation in any real world supply chain. No single independent actor is paying all of the taxes throughout the entire supply chain. It's always the person receiving the revenue that is responsible for their portion of the tax.   Buy some resources from someone on the LM -- the seller pays the marketplace royalty,Take those resources and craft items -- pay the crafting fee. This is a manufacturing cost for access to the facility, not a tax,Use those crafted components to build a ship and sell it on the marketplace -- again, paying a fee, but generating revenue, Manufacturers need to account for these costs. And the economy has proven exceptionally good at finding price equalibrium naturally.

author: Emperor ===[]
content:
Nobody’s compelled to join Star Atlas—we all chose this, 6% Local Markets fee and all. It’s not a hurdle to duck; it’s what powers the DAO—power to the people, not a loophole playground. Your NFTs are yours, sure, but trading them on Local Markets isn’t a right; it’s a service with a 6% cut to keep the DAO breathing. OTC saps ATLAS from that—dev, growth, the stuff we’re here for. Swags nailed it: it taps every POLIS staker and our shot at something real.
Fee-free P2P? Tempting, but it’d bleed the treasury dry faster than Solana fees hit small players—grind loops if you’ve got time, no pass needed. Redistribution’s a fantasy for Earth’s bureaucracies, not Star Atlas. We’re here for liberty, not leveling—nobody’s compelled to play, and nobody’s owed a handout. The ATLAS Locker’s the play—lock in, ease fees, rise up, all your call. Fees rough? Adapt or walk—nobody’s forcing your wallet open.
We signed up for an economy, not a barter sim. Those rules—fees included—are the deal we made. Liberty’s in owning it, not rewriting it. That’s Star Atlas to me.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Not sure if this is supposed to be inspiring to me, but it isn't. Naturally we want more players. But if the way to accomplish that is through system exploitation, we're doing something wrong. Personally, I see a lot of ways to engage profitably. The total gross fee on the economy is roughly 10%. That means 90% of your activity is captured by you. And the rest goes 85% to the DAO for further ecosystem development, and 15% to ATMTA to supplement game dev. If the only way you can play is by stealing money from every other player, you are probably doing something wrong.

author: Icy
content: I hope you are joking. I really do. There is such a MASSIVE opportunity for everyone. Opportunity for whales, minnows, capitalist's and communists alike. Create a pip, have a solid idea and a plan to implement it and also account for the oversite. Rally the troops, buy a ship, mine some shit, post the pip and fight through the bullshit process. Be a part of making the bullshit process easier. Write PIPS after pips and gather support for the change you "want". Literally anything except sit around a bitch that someone should do something. Keyboard cowboys won't cut it in the world we are voluntarily a part of. Run ideas through Grok, IDK, do something. God damn man SO much opportunity to not stand there and complain. We are literally a part of an OPPORTUNITY to create a new world. I am not by any stretchy of the imagination saying any of this is easy FWIW. Anything worth doing is most likely going to be crazy hard (just ask Swags) and full of setbacks.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I am not acting out of spite or hate. I am talking from the perspective of a new player that we want to keep and is not a spreadsheet warrior, polis or atlas locker user that just wants to have fun and play the beginningof the game. They won't see DAO fees as opportunity they will see how much it cuts their work and eventually spiral to create a spreadsheet until they quit. I know DAO fees are part of the plan since the beginning and I am not saying all of them but we don't need them on performing every action of the game, cutting and cutting everytime you move. Gotta maximize player growth and experience. Also if bad reviews catch fire it will be much harder to grab new players.

author: [AEP] Siphaawal
content:
We all come for the love of Star Atlas - to cut the tension, here is a poem to hopefully bring a smile to you all

In the Star Atlas realm, where crypto dreams ignite,
Some whisper of fees that dim the market's light.
Crafting fees so high, they push traders astray,
Seeking OTC paths, in the shadows they play.

But ATMTA stands firm, closing every gap,
Ensuring every fee is paid, closing the loophole trap.
When fairness prevails and the system is tight,
The game reaches its zenith, and all soar to new heights.

Tokens of ATLAS and POLIS, in value they climb,
A future well secured, a victory sublime.

Gotta maximize player growth and experience. Also if bad reviews catch fire it will be much harder to grab new players.

author: [-U-] OrionorbitMUD
content: one major difference is that you have to freight with the galactic market.  with local markets no freighting is required, or less freighting.  the other major difference is the game elements.   The galactic market is "out of game". And there are no game mechanics with galactic market.  Whereas local markets are IN GAME.   All interactions are happening in game.   So when people are doing OTC they are essentially bypassing game mechanics AND bypassing the fee.     It also breaks economic balance by giving players the ability to trade in local markets without the corresponding fee or the exportation to the CSS and GM.   e.g. the carefully defined balance Chris has created.   It also hurts all other players who choose not to abuse the situation and creates a negative situation where other players have to engage in abusive behavior to compete.

author: defiantwyvern
content:
you can fix the GM issue (for anyone that's buying assets for use in the SA ecosystem anyway).
Add import or export fees/taxes/regulations.,All trades at CSS take place as local market trades at each CSS market.,Effectively get rid of the GM ... maybe need to consider where SA sells primary assets, though.,
People would be free to trade outside of SA ecosystem (e.g. Wallet <-> Wallet), but if those assets want to see any use, they have to cross an SA border at some point. That point, ATMTA/SA could control.

author: defiantwyvern
content: Just like a country can't easily control taxes on transactions that happen in other countries. But it can impose tariffs or taxes as goods enter and leave the country, assuming it has good border controls. e.g. customs.

author: defiantwyvern
content: the LM issue could be fixed by imposing an adjusting floating value fee per item (similar to crafting fees). That would need to be market-specific and would require constant adjustment to market conditions. But it would fix the issue - would require all fees to be paid, even for "cheap OTC" deals. OTC deals only avoid the tax b/c it effectively hides the true value of the transaction. But the transaction is still under the control of SAGE, so there is a chance to enforce it there instead of needing to retroactively police it.

author: defiantwyvern
content: We have discussed import/export fees quite considerably internally. And yes, it would be a potential solution to out-of-game OTC trading fee circumvention. Not without it's own issues though: Adding more fees to the game, which serves as an even higher barrier to entry. Are the fees on import, export, or both? As we've seen over the weekend, people aren't particularly fond of fees in general,Determining the value of the assets on the fly (at time of entry or exit): use VWAP? use some estimate based on current trade value? Again, calculating that on the fly at the time of a transaction is challenging,User needs to hold some amount of liquid tokens like USDC or ATLAS to pay the fee. Ships aren't fractional. Can't just take a "portion" of the ship/fleet at the Port of Entry, so those fees need to be paid in a fungible asset,Support issues: people uncertain why they can't get assets in or out of the game, adding more friction to the entire process, edit: 5. These fees, btw, penalize everyone, when it is really just a select group of people avoiding paying marketplace fees  While it could work, requires us developing a lot of logic and adding a considerable amount of friction to the game.

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content:
This situation is truly tragicomic. In the system you have built, external traders are conducting ten times the volume of transactions compared to your market. Yet, when someone suggests ways to prevent this, they are accused of theft.

It is widely accepted that a system needs to charge fees to sustain itself. However, if the tax you impose directly impacts my earnings, then I’m sorry, but I will seek alternative solutions elsewhere. That is exactly why players are looking for ways to trade outside your market instead of dealing with your high fees.

I have mentioned this before, but I was attacked for saying it. Today, I am saying it again: The most reasonable solution to this issue is to lower the fees.

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content:
It is worth repeating this: Let’s assume an average of 100 transactions take place daily, and we know that around 70% of these are conducted in OTC markets. If you lower your transaction fees, you can be certain that those running OTC markets will shut them down. This is because everyone understands that no one would hesitate to pay a fair fee for the service you provide.

The real issue here is how many of these OTC transactions you can attract to your marketplace. This is not a difficult problem to solve. You attempted to counteract this by activating local markets, but in doing so, you ended up shooting yourselves in the foot. Since people didn’t want to pay high fees, they simply sold their assets at lower prices and established a new OTC platform based on mutual trust. In the end, you were the ones who suffered.

I had already proposed a solution to this months ago: implementing a floor price to prevent such issues. While this is not an absolute solution, I was confident that it could provide at least a partial resolution to your revenue concerns.

content: As for your proposal to tax crafting, it is perhaps a bit beyond my ability to explain on a discord server, but in academic economics the following is tought: sales taxes are much preferable to any other kind of taxes because they distort the economy the least. The solution you propose is closer to something like a production tax and much more distortive, ie leading to lower GDP for star atlas

I don’t have the minimum reliability on economic side but my 2 cents would be the min max strategy that Zamsor proposed. 
Do not let people to chose an order and buy/sell on best prices, just like a normal order book works.
Min effort, max results.

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content:
One of the things that frustrates me the most is "being more royalist than the king." There is an armor meant to protect the city, and it must never be breached. However, due to certain internal conflicts within the city, the armor keeps getting punctured because of loopholes in the system.

At the same time, it’s truly a tragedy to see the very people who preach about how the armor must remain intact being the ones who are actually breaking it. If you are going to enforce a system, start by following it yourself. And instead of lecturing those who offer solutions, perhaps you should take a look in the mirror first.

"Stand in front of the mirror and ask yourself: Why did I do it?"

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Guys, I do appreciate all of the ideas, but we're not actually short on ways to fix these issues. The proposed solutions don't account for developer time and resources to actually build out temporary solutions. Creating a system that calculates, stores, and translates marketplace fees per item, updating on the fly, is not a simple task. Which is why we've deprioritized fixing these systems that are exploitable and focused on new feature development.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: We are only discussing the Resources category and export fees to discourage OTC trading. All other assets can remain in the GM.,Value could be determined as it is today, using a combination of averages. There is no need to use VWAP since this process only applies to Resources.,Users already need to hold some SOL and/or ATLAS for transactions. No change is needed here since we are only referring to Resources.,There would be no need to remove Resources from the game. ATLAS (or even USDC) can flow freely after the sale.,There would be no penalty. Unless players want to store Resources in their wallet, there is no need to remove them from the game.,NOTE: Crafted ships or parts could receive special treatment or could optionally be sent to SA to be sold on the GM (for a fee).  This proposal considers the limited human capital available for SA and aims to minimize frictional loops within the game.   CC: @defiantwyvern

reply.preview: We are only discussing the Resources category and export fees to discourage OTC trading. All other assets can remain in the GM. , Value could be determined as it is today, using a combination of averages. There is no need to use VWAP since this process only applies to Resources. , Users already need to hold some SOL and/or ATLAS for transactions. No change is needed here since we are only referring to Resources. , There would be no need to remove Resources from the game. ATLAS (or even USDC) can flow freely after the sale. , There would be no penalty. Unless players want to store Resources in their wallet, there is no need to remove them from the game. , NOTE: Crafted ships or parts could receive special treatment or could optionally be sent to SA to be sold on the GM (for a fee). This proposal considers the limited human capital available for SA and aims to minimize frictional loops within the game. CC: @defiantwyvern

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: ~Regarding OTC ships...one step at a time (separate plan).  ~The plan is really proposing that Resources stay within game, stay within Galia. Why remove the Resources from the game when their utility shines inside the game? Value can follow the free Local Markets at the bases.  ~Yes, with Resources flowing within the game, some players may be tempted to engage in interfaction exchanges. However, by restricting P2P transfers and/or taxing Resource transfers (excluding loot), this behavior could be minimized or even eliminated.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content:
I can say with 10000% conviction that there were no leaks. It's likely related to what Jaywalk said re: FICs but I'll also add that it could also be related to Frameworks and the LP system.  With GTs and Mambas gone you can only pivot to either.  LP is by far the simpler engine if you are waiting for the next mamba recipe.

It's also entirely possible that these whales are reading the market.  If GTs and Mambas are gone for an extended period then it's natural that iron ore prices would decline.  Which means there is a buying opportunity to take advantage of before the next release.

author: 《VΛ》Agent_Solace
content: Eveeye doesn’t need infra for routing transactions, they can go directly to RPCs and that’s why they have a higher success rate. This is not scalable though.  Since we have to process a lot more txs across all our programs we need to have infrastructure behind, where some transactions are currently failing because we recently migrated servers. (Starcomm)
